quote: Isn't the implicit assumption underlying such a measure that all problems are equally relevant? Yes; it's sometimes called the impartial culture assumption. In spite of its name, which almost makes it sound as if the investigator themself is unbiased and open-minded — so how could one object to it — it's an assumption that some people find a little problematic. It would make more sense to place more weight on problems that are more likely. To formalize this idea, however, you'd need to write down a probability distribution from which problems would be drawn. Unfortunately, specific distributional assumptions are hard to justify. The tradition is to work with uniform distributions.

stance.implicit_norm: any 'objective' measure hides a choice of distribution; confessing the distribution is part of intellectual honesty
stance.trigger: evaluation of an axiom across a domain
stance.verdict_pattern: if you measure %-satisfaction, ask: 'where does the uniform come from?' The default is often unjustified.
stance.rhetorical_device: mock-innocent inversion of 'impartial culture' — turning a flattering name into a confession of bias

counter_example: if you compute '% of preference profiles where VCG is ex-post IC' and report it as 78%, you have implicitly assumed a uniform distribution over profiles. But the real distribution (from real auctions) might give 92%. The two numbers mean very different things.
origin_story: Thomson is calling out a soft spot in his own methodology: parameterization requires picking a distribution, and the field's default is 'uniform' — which is as much a model choice as the axiom itself. The remark appears in Ch.10 §10.2 right after introducing the parameterization method — it's the author hedging his own proposal before moving on.

applicability.use_when: value_evaluator or axiom_deriver is computing a quantitative trade-off
applicability.use_how: always accompany a numeric verdict with: (a) the assumed distribution, (b) a sentence acknowledging that the distribution itself is a choice

process_meta.[HUNCH]: 从 Thomson 风格抽出的 tacit judgment
